title: Those Who Cannot Climb a Roof and Catch a Hen!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which clear OCR character, punctuation, numeral, and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: On 13 February 2014, presenting the AIADMK government's Budget in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly, today's Chief Minister O. Panneerselvam read on page 31: “I am happy to inform this august House that, to give further impetus to industrial growth and infrastructure in our state, this government will hold an International Investors' Conference in Chennai this coming October. A sum of Rs 100 crore has been allocated for it in this Budget.”

That announcement was read in last year's Budget. But was the investors' conference held in October as the Minister told the Assembly? No. If this is the fate of an announcement in the Budget itself, can any other announcement have meaning? The AIADMK rulers must explain what sort of wisdom it is to announce a conference and then postpone it without holding it even five months later.

Back in 2012, they made a gigantic announcement that Memoranda of Understanding would be signed with twelve companies in a single day, creating jobs for 36,855 people and bringing factories with an investment of Rs 20,925 crore. The newspapers carried it prominently. We asked the government to tell the people whether all twelve companies had brought their investments and begun operations in Tamil Nadu, and whether 36,855 people had actually found work in them. The rulers did not open their mouths. They could speak only if something had happened!

On another occasion, reports said agreements worth Rs 5,081 crore had been signed with sixteen companies, but those companies had still not begun production. Last year's Budget also said MoUs had been signed for a total proposed investment of Rs 26,625 crore, of which only Rs 10,660 crore had actually been invested, creating an additional 10,022 jobs.

Chief Minister Panneerselvam later announced that an investors' conference would be held in May 2015. I asked then what benefit could come from holding it in May 2015 when only one year would remain before the Assembly general election. There was no answer to that either.

They advertised grandly that agreements worth Rs 18,000 crore would be signed with seven companies at the curtain-raiser held in Chennai on 13 February 2015 for the May conference. Yet that signing did not take place there at all. Is that not shameful?

Tamil Nadu's Industries Minister said: “They invite people to Andhra Pradesh by offering land at a low price and tax concessions. Likewise, whenever the Andhra Chief Minister travels through Chennai, he calls our industrialists and offers them low-priced land.” That statement itself reveals Tamil Nadu's condition. When another state's Chief Minister comes into a state they govern, holds a meeting and invites its investors away, they ought to feel ashamed. What is gained by pouncing upon us instead?

I also said that, when the Karnataka Chief Minister comes to a Tamil Nadu city, gathers Tamil Nadu's industrialists and invites them to establish industries in Karnataka, even an ordinary industrialist can understand what it means.

We did not invent the claim that the Karnataka Chief Minister held a meeting in Coimbatore and invited Tamil Nadu investors. Dinamalar is no DMK newspaper. It is a paper that severely criticises the DMK. On 22 January it published a report headed: “Tamil Nadu Pushes Away; Karnataka Attracts: Rs 12,000-Crore Investment Slips from Our Hands.”

Speaking in Coimbatore, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said her three-year government had signed MoUs with thirty-three Indian and multinational companies for investment of Rs 31,706 crore. Can the government say how many of those thirty-three companies have actually begun operations in Tamil Nadu? Agreements can be signed with many companies. They are useful only when industries truly begin!

Reports now say Panneerselvam held discussions with Britain's Trade Minister about greater investment by British companies in Tamil Nadu, and with the Governor of South Carolina in the United States about further investment here. We do not know what became of those discussions either.

They announced that the Global Investors Meet would be held on 23 and 24 May, organised a curtain-raiser, advertised agreements with investors, and failed even to complete those. Meanwhile, officials reportedly marched off abroad to attract foreign participants. Tamil Nadu's Industries Minister said many foreign industrialists would attend the Chennai conference and that it could bring investment of Rs 1 lakh crore. Only days after he said so, the government announced yesterday that the conference had again been postponed.

A consultation to invite local industrialists was reportedly held in Salem yesterday, attended by the Industries Minister, the Highways Minister and the Industries Department's Special Secretary. The Ministers spoke there as though the conference would occur in May. Yet, without even their knowing, Chennai announced that it had been postponed to September. What an example of the Cabinet's coordination!

It is being said that the conference is repeatedly postponed so that it can take place after Jayalalithaa is acquitted in the disproportionate-assets appeal and is again Chief Minister. Nor, it is said, is this the only event affected. Although some government projects are complete, their inaugurations are reportedly being delayed because they must not be opened while Jayalalithaa is not Chief Minister. Hundreds of buses purchased for the State transport corporations are said to be lying unused and causing heavy losses because they have not been inaugurated. Metro Rail projects likewise remain unopened.
The reason now offered for postponing the conference is the severe heat in May.

Did they discover only now that May is fiercely hot? When they first announced a May conference, did this government not know it? They have postponed it to September, when rain may be severe. If judgment in Jayalalithaa's case has not come by then, will they postpone the investors' conference yet again, citing September rain?

The government claims that foreign investors are extremely eager to invest in our state and that this became clear at seminars held abroad. If investors show such interest, is it proper for the Tamil Nadu government to delay the conference? After completing four years in office, the AIADMK government has postponed the date of the Global Investors Meet for a third time. Does that not reveal the depth of its concern for industrial growth?

Yet the AIADMK rulers speak as though they alone bear responsibility for Tamil Nadu's industrial development. Is there any difference between this government and those loud boasters of whom it is said: “They cannot climb onto a roof and catch a hen, but claim they will tear open the sky and show the road to Vaikuntha”?
With affection,
M.K.
23 April 2015
